data.title: Intuitive Machines: From Lunar Lander To Space Infrastructure Play
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (LUNR) is transitioning from a company that received its revenues from one-time contracts, to a more sustainable model with recurring revenue potential. This creates opportunity for long-term investors to buy a next-generation space company at a valuation where this potential isn't entirely priced in. With the win of the Near Space Network Services (NSNS) contract from NASA back in 2024, the company now also has infrastructure optionality. The contract has a total value of $4.82 billion and includes a five-year base period and a five-year option. It also positions them in the communications and navigation segment of the Artemis system instead of only being a transportation provider. This changes Intuitive's story from landing on the moon to developing and maintaining lunar communication, data relay services, and navigation, which has more long-term value.

Intuitive also has strong financials with $943 million in backlog and given guidance to nearly 5x revenue in 2026. In addition, the company provided information indicating that it expects to generate positive adjusted EBITDA during 2026, showing operating leverage as the NSNS project ramps up and other projects continue. The company also acquired Lanteris in early 2026 for $800 million, expanding Intuitive beyond lunar delivery services and allowing it to develop spacecraft and work in national security. NASA also awarded Intuitive a $180.4 million contract under IM-5 as well as a previously funded Lunar Terrain Vehicle contract worth $30 million, further solidifying the company in its role for lunar logistics and infrastructure. Overall, the company appears less speculative, with opportunities for recurring revenue and multiple ways to benefit from space exploration through both defense and commercial contracts. This is something I don't believe is fully factored into today's valuation, which is why I rate the stock a Buy.

The $4.82 Billion NSNS Contract

The key factor in the company's transformation is the NASA NSNS contract, awarded to Intuitive Machines in September of 2024 for up to $4.82 billion over a base term of five years with an additional five-year option. As opposed to other contracts for landing hardware, this contract is for infrastructure, where Intuitive is building a satellite constellation that will support lunar relay communication, navigation services, and data transmission for all of the NASA Artemis missions. A mission like this also paves the way for potential commercial and international missions, who could also use that infrastructure.

This changes Intuitive from a company earning money each time it successfully lands a spacecraft to one that will earn recurring service income so long as there are users for its infrastructure (which will be the case for years under the Artemis timeline). Whoever builds out the communication and navigation layer for lunar space will build the first lunar internet, making them the toll road for every mission after. With NASA looking for a long-term lunar base, this change should not be taken lightly. This is a key aspect I believe is being overlooked in today's valuation, where growth potential appears better than what the market is currently pricing in.

Revenue Updates

Intuitive Machines produced $210.1 million in FY 2025 revenue which represents a ~7.9% decline YoY. Additionally, the company achieved a 19% gross margin, along with a backlog of $943 million. Revenue guidance for FY 2026 appears strong, with a range of $0.9-1.0 billion, representing a near five-fold increase over last year's revenue. With ~65% of this guidance being supported by contracted backlog, it represents one of the most visible near-term ramps in the aerospace industry.

This large increase in revenue is expected to be driven by NSNS service revenue, Lanteris defense contributions, and continued delivery of CLPS missions. The company expects to report positive adjusted EBITDA for the first time in FY 2026, indicating that revenue is finally outpacing fixed costs. Expansion of gross margins to 19% in 2025 and projected positive adjusted EBITDA show that as service revenue grows there is operational leverage in this revenue expansion. This marks an inflection point for the company, where the pathway to profitability is becoming increasingly realistic.

Nova-D and the Heavy Lunar Logistics Market

Nova-D's ability to carry substantial payload weights places Intuitive at the forefront of contenders for the potential $4.6 billion Lunar Terrain Vehicle (LTV) contract. This LTV contract would provide for the delivery and operation of a crewed rover for the astronauts working on the lunar surface. The company was awarded a $30 million NASA contract as prime contractor for the LTV feasibility study. Winning this LTV contract would be transformative with respect to an already-full order book for the Company.

In March 2026, NASA selected Intuitive to receive a $180.4 million CLPS contract for the IM-5 mission that utilizes the Nova-D lander. This could help their odds for the large LTV contract, with their vehicles already being used by NASA. However, regardless of whether the LTV Contract is won, the Nova-D capability increases the addressable market for heavy scale lunar logistics significantly over the Odysseus-class lander. This provides optionality for the company, where if the $4.6 billion contract is won, it could potentially cause a re-rating in the company's valuation.

Defense Diversification

The $800 million completed purchase of Lanteris Space Systems in January 2026 added a national security component to the company that has the potential to alter the company's risk profile. The Lanteris 1300 series of spacecraft platforms will be able to provide cislunar space domain awareness and orbital maneuverability that directly aligns with the US Space Force's objective to monitor and operate in the space between the Earth and the Moon. The combined entity produced over $850 million in annual revenue and had a $920 million backlog as of the announcement date, while also maintaining positive adjusted EBITDA.

The Department of Defense (DoD) has begun treating cislunar space as a strategically important domain that requires a persistent presence, surveillance, and operational capability. With the acquisition, there is no other company that offers a vertically-integrated solution capable of delivering payload to the lunar surface while providing a cislunar space domain awareness platform. The DoD also represents a more durable customer base compared to customers reliant on NASA exploration budgets, which can sometimes be reduced due to government budget changes. This creates a more reliable stream of income for Intuitive, reducing a key source of risk for the company. This combined with upside from large NASA contract creates a favorable risk/reward for long-term investors, where potential growth may not be priced in.

Valuation

At a per share price of ~$28 and a ~$6 billion market cap, Intuitive trades ~4.7x forward sales. Established defense and space infrastructure prime contractors such as L3 Harris (LHX) and Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH) are trading at ~0.5-3.0x forward sales; however, both are mature, low-growth businesses. Defense tech high-growth companies with multi-billion dollar contract backlogs and strong forward revenue growth often see 5-10x forward revenue multiples. Intuitive is trading at the midpoint of these ranges, and it also guides to positive adjusted EBITDA, holds a $943 million backlog, and is part of multi-year US infrastructure mandates.

Applying a 7x forward revenue multiple on the midpoint of revenue guidance of $950 million brings the company's valuation to $7.6 billion. When divided by shares outstanding of ~218 million (calculated by dividing market cap by share price after acquisition completion), price per share is ~$34.94, which is my 12-month price target. I believe this is warranted as the backlog starts turning to revenue and the EBITDA inflection becomes reality. This also prices in the new risk profile of Intuitive, with steady defense revenues, and optionality for contracts like NASA's $4.6 billion LTV award.

Risks to the Thesis

Dilution is a risk, with Intuitive not yet profitable. Although positive adjusted EBITDA is expected in 2026, any delay in this timeline could lead to dilution.

With guidance expecting 5x in revenue YoY, execution is another risk. If Intuitive faces delays on schedules and milestones, it could cause them to miss estimates.

NASA budget pressure is probably the largest risk, with the Artemis program historically facing scheduling issues. Any change in this could affect Intuitive's contracts and how soon the revenue is realized. However, the DoD revenue should help offset this risk, which provides a steadier stream of cash than NASA contracts.

Takeaway

As Intuitive turns into an execution-driven story, it suggests that the company's opportunity is much larger and much more stable than ever before. It is now composed of communications infrastructure, heavy lunar logistics, and national security programs, which diversify its cash flows with revenue from more stable streams like defense. The NSNS contract also changes the business model to recurring revenue streams similar to those associated with infrastructure, changing the company's story to one that is not fully dependent on one-time contracts. These two elements provide a base for future success by providing less dependency on a single mission outcome and aligning itself with the long-term goals of the US in cislunar space.

If Intuitive Machines can execute on transitioning from backlog to sustainable revenue growth and demonstrate that their infrastructure strategy can scale, the stock could potentially command a re-rating. With a strong NASA partnership, the company has opportunity as a next-generation space business. With operations in key areas of exploration, communications, and defense, I see this as a strong possibility. This new mix of growth verticals makes Intuitive a compelling opportunity at current levels, which is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $34.94.

data.title: Netflix: Attractive Dip Buying Opportunity After Latest U.S. Price Increases
data.description:
The beginning of the Q1 earnings season is upon us, and we finally have more company-specific catalysts to focus our attention on beyond the volatile backdrop of the Iran conflict. For the past two quarters now, Netflix (NFLX) has been a major earnings disappointer to kick off the tech earnings season.

Like last quarter, investors zeroed in on Netflix’s implied guidance deceleration for Q2. Q1 brought additional complications from the exit of Reed Hastings, the company’s visionary co-founder, from his role as chairman of Netflix’s board. So far, shares of Netflix are down ~10% post earnings, though the stock remains marginally positive from a YTD standpoint as investors are still cheering the termination of the deal to buy Warner Bros.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Netflix in January, upgrading the stock to a "Buy" immediately after its Q4 earnings disappointment when it dropped to the low $80s. Since then, my position has already driven a healthy return, but I’ll cut to the chase: I’m taking advantage of yet another post-earnings slide to add to my bullish bet on the leading streaming giant, specifically by selling short-dated puts on the company. Netflix remains down ~30% from its all-time highs above $130 notched last year, and that gap represents substantial rebound opportunity, especially as Netflix continues to flex its pricing power and builds out its advertising business. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating on this name.

Q2 implied guidance deceleration is no major concern

Broadly, Netflix’s post-Q1 drop was accentuated by two main factors: Reed Hastings’s exit, plus the implied revenue deceleration in Netflix’s Q2 guidance outlook. We think the Hastings news is a temporary headwind that added an additional overhang on a stock that was already leaning pessimistically, and it should fade shortly. The deceleration topic, however, is one we can peel back in more detail. Take a look at Netflix’s earnings summary below:

For Q2, Netflix is guiding to $12.57 billion in revenue, which represents 13.5% y/y growth. Wall Street consensus, however, had been hoping for $12.63 billion in revenue, or 50bps stronger at 14.0% y/y growth.

The bigger conversation beyond the miss, however, was the 270bps in deceleration versus a 16.2% growth rate in Q1. This prompted fear in the market that this deceleration signified, finally, an erosion in Netflix’s pricing power and that the company was finally experiencing churn.

There are several reasons I think this argument is misguided and overblown.

First, we consider management’s own characterization of customer behavior post price change (the hike was announced late March and kicked off for the April billing cycle which varies by customer, so presumably the company already had an early read on demand by the time of the earnings call). In the shareholder letter accompanying the earnings, management wrote: “Our recent price changes have gone well, reflecting the strong value we provide members.”

Second, consider the history of Netflix’s price increases. In 2025, the company implemented a price jump of $15.49 to $17.99 for Standard plans (a $2.50, or 16% jump) and from $6.99 to $7.99 for Ad-Supported plans (a 14% jump). Timing wise, this move was announced in January 2025 and implemented February, partially benefiting Q1’25.

This year’s price increases, meanwhile, were a $2 bump (11%) to the new $19.99 price for Standard, and a $1 bump (13%) to $8.99 for Ad-Supported. Though this year’s price increases were an “unexpected surprise,” they are lower in percentage magnitude for both of the company’s primary plans, and lower in both percentage and nominal dollars for Standard.

For the first half of Q1’26 (from January to mid-February, before lapping the $17.99 price point), Netflix benefited from roughly half a quarter where U.S. prices were ~15% higher than last year. The upcoming Q2’26, meanwhile, faces the full burden of comping against last year’s ~15% price increases, which can only be partially offset by this year’s slightly more modest increase. Expecting acceleration here, in my view, was unreasonable, and importantly, I don’t think at all that deceleration means churn is rising.

Thirdly, note that Netflix’s gap to guidance is more or less a repeat of what happened in Q4. At that time in January, the company had guided to 15.3% revenue growth for Q1 (vs. Street expectations for 15.5%), and actually the company delivered 16.2% growth, a full point above the original outlook. No matter how the company stresses its guidance is a true “likely case” that is also used for internal planning purposes, history suggests that Netflix usually beats its expectations, so there is no need to sell off the stock ~10% on a 50bps guidance miss just yet.

Breakup fee, advertising opportunity

Since last quarter, there are also a number of other reasons to remain long on Netflix. We cheer the termination of the Warner Bros. deal.

Financially, the end of the deal is a major plus for Netflix shareholders. The kicker here of course is the $2.8 billion breakup fee (pre-tax) that Netflix is collecting from Warner Bros. For sizing purposes, on a pre tax basis this amount is worth 30% of the company’s FY25 FCF of $9.5 billion.

Operationally, remaining as a standalone, Netflix also offers many advantages. The Warner deal would have incurred a mountain of debt to cover its $82.7 billion all-cash offer, which may have hampered the company’s ability to keep investing in its growth initiatives (gaming, live events and programming, and advertising). We’re also not sure if Netflix could really have driven top-line synergies by driving Warner’s assets, mainly HBO Max, into its portfolio. Increasingly, Netflix is trying to position itself as a hub of premier flagship content like Bridgerton (where the company reported the recently released fourth season amassed a staggering 94 million views). The company would have had to offer bundled pricing to package the two plans, which may have led to revenue loss between the two brands (or choosing one streaming service over the other).

We also remain encouraged by Netflix noting that its advertising business remains well on track to double y/y to $3 billion. The company’s ad plan offers a nice bulwark to catch members who may have churned on rising prices.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

I welcome the opportunity to add to my Netflix long at a rare discount to its historical multiples. For FY26 and FY27, respectively, Wall Street is expecting Netflix to hit $3.56 and $3.85 in pro forma EPS, respectively. At current share prices near $95, this positions Netflix’s valuation at:

  • 26.7x FY26 P/E
  • 24.7x FY27 P/E

While not a value stock, Netflix trades competitively with the rest of large-cap tech and below its peak valuations last year.

Of course, there are risks here to consider. We don’t like the fact that Netflix no longer reports subscriber data. We fully expect Netflix to be able to surmount its expectations of >$3 billion in ad revenue this year, but what will be more unclear is the extent to which this success may be driven by customers mixing down from higher-tier subscription plans due to price increases. We also think, from a longer-term perspective, Netflix continues to compete heavily against “free” screen time from the likes of TikTok and YouTube.

That said, there’s a lot to like here as Netflix still executes at double-digit growth. Stay long here and "Buy" the dip.

data.title: Lightwave Logic: A Materials Bet On The Bottleneck In AI Optics
data.description:
The best way to introduce Lightwave Logic (LWLG) is to clarify that this is not a photonics company, and is not really trying to sell transceivers and photonics engines. What they are really trying to sell is a semiconductor compatible electro-optic polymer material system. Actually, they are trying to sell the IP and know-how needed to make the material work inside real packaging flows in foundries.

I was perhaps a bit too abstract. A simpler way to put it is to say that the company wants its polymer to become the layer that allows for light to switch faster using less power inside AI data centers.

The recent price action suggests the company is now being priced more as a platform option and less as an interesting science project, and this is what makes this stock interesting. The price is breaking out at the same time its IP seems to be gaining traction among the DC buildout crowd.

What the company actually does

Lightwave Logic develops electro-optic polymers for high speed optical modulation in data communications. Modulators work like a flashlight switch inside optical links, and they need to do two things particularly well, they need to flip really fast, while using very little energy.

The hard part about this is that in the end, it is a materials engineering exercise, and the whole thing will only works if the polymer is reliable, manufacturable using standard semiconductor tools and designable by others instead of bespoke engineering (i.e., not scalable).

Business model

The company is basically a materials company that monetizes through material supply and IP licensing. They sell the polymer for production, then they license the IP and reference design so that clients can tape out polymer-enabled modulators in foundries. Finally, they also get royalties tied to production volumes.

The beauty of this business model is that if the various foundries adopt Lightwave’s solution, the economics can scale without them having to own a single foundry. The drawback is that the business can look stuck for a long time, even as they are making real technical progress. A full cycle might need 24 months to play out. A long time for investors.

Investment thesis

The bull case for this stock is no longer optics growth inside data centers, the more refined thesis is now “as AI DCs scale with tighter power budgets, modulator technology has to be both fast and power cheap, and the winners will be able to integrate cleanly silicon photonics with foundry and packaging ecosystems.”

In other words, silicon photonics has been seeing significant adoption because it works well in foundry and scales well enough throughout the supply chain. Obviously, there is an item list to clear. For starters, the polymer needs to perform well at scale with wafer level repeatability and yield. On the other hand, the reliability needs to be flawless under temperature and light exposure or else it won’t work. In essence, this needs to be reliable at scale, while being just part of the process routine for customers.

Valuation

The company still has no meaningful revenue, and there are no revenue estimates available from analysts. Therefore, the best proxy available is P/BV to try to capture a value for the economic assets. I know this is a poor proxy, but it is all we have for now.

A look into the valuation comps tells us that the market is actually assigning a large value to the company’s optionality relative to its net assets. In other words, the company’s books are mostly cash and working capital, while investors are actually paying for the probability of the polymer IP and process know how to mature into an industrial category.

At 25x P/BV the market is basically saying “we are valuing this as an optics platform option.” And the rate of change in the last few months matters more because the book value changes slowly. Therefore, bearing in mind the company raised cash recently, the rise in the last few months is a hint that the market has been upgrading the company’s probability to be successful.

We can’t really go much beyond the idea that the market is repricing the company’s chances. Directly comparing against the peers is hard on this metric. In any case if we see the company’s P/BV drifting lower, it will likely mean the market upgraded on momentum and now is reverting back to mean.

Risks

The thesis relies on the assumption that the company’s EO polymer can clear the long-tail reliability problems that have been an historical obstacle that has limited adoption. If the product fails to achieve long term performance with reliability, the market will never make it a standard solution for the industry and the share price will get stuck.

The company’s modulators need to be “selectable” for standard industry workflows, if they remain a custom solution forever, they will get lots of pilots but few standardizations that drive real volume. In this industry, good enough coupled with easy to scale many times wins the day due to convenience.

Additionally, given the company is still operating at a loss, and the schedule to cash-flow break even is not clear, investors risk further dilution or even worse, bankruptcy.

Wrapping up

As AI data centers push lane rates higher and energy budgets down, modulators become a constraint, and Lightwave logic has a very interesting technological solution that addresses exactly that. In this segment, the value is unlocked when a solution graduates from a lab curiosity to a scalable solution. This matters because it explains why the stock price can move before any news about firm revenue is out.

The recent breakout in the stock price is very noticeable. The market seems to be graduating the stock by assigning it a higher probability of the company’s polymer to make it through the selection process. When a pre-revenue company like Lightwave breaks out from a long consolidated base, it usually means that what is changing is not fundamentals today, but how they will look in 18 months.

So, yes, Lightwave Logic is still a speculative long on the idea that their polymer can become standard for silicon photonics in AI networks, but the stock breakout reflects a higher belief that the company is on a good track to unlock high margins materials business coupled with an IP/royalty stream.

Given that several bottlenecks are constraining the AI DC buildout, I don’t find it hard to believe that the company has a valuable solution. My sole issue is that for now, it is very hard to quantify the potential for this company. We know that the management team believes the serviceable addressable market is $1 to $2.5 billion for 2028.

I find this a bit underwhelming given that the company already has a $1.9 billion market cap. Given that I lack a proper yardstick to have a rough measure of the company’s potential, I will remain on the sidelines, but I will keep an eye on the company.

data.title: Tesla's Earnings Reaction May Be More Subdued Than Usual
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is expected to report earnings after the close of trading on April 22, with the options market pricing in a post-earnings move of about 5%. That is a surprisingly modest move for a stock that has delivered much larger swings in the past.

Earnings for the first quarter are expected to rise 38% year over year to $0.37 per share, with revenue growth of 17.5% to $22.7 billion and gross margins of 17.8%. For the full year, the company is expected to have revenue growth of about 8.5% and gross margins of 18.2%, while CapEx is expected to more than double to $18.2 billion from $8.5 billion a year ago.

How Much Is Priced In

The stock has fallen by around 30% since late January, raising the question of how much of the company's growth prospects and increased spending plans are already priced into the shares. At least on the surface, the sharp decline appears more attributable to the increased spending plans.

Based on the options market, even with what appear to be healthy results, it seems the market has already priced much of it. Currently, 1-week at-the-money implied volatility for Tesla is near 60%, and that may continue to rise into results, but that is a fairly low value historically for the stock. This would suggest that the premiums for both puts and calls are not as high as they usually are heading into results, at least as of the close of trading on April 20.

Option Positioning

It implies limited premium decay following the results and suggests the market expects shares to remain in a potentially tight trading range and not see a big post-earnings move.

From a positioning standpoint, there is a large amount of call gamma built up around $400, which means that unless the stock can get over that level of resistance, it is likely to face selling pressure there. On the downside, put gamma near $380 could offer support.

With the stock in a positive gamma regime, dealer hedging flows are likely to reinforce this range, as buyers of pullbacks and sellers of rallies.

The Impacts of Positioning

At least from an options perspective, the premium decay may not be significant if Tesla's implied volatility falls, given how low it currently is. For example, a $390 call expiring on Friday, April 24 has a premium of about $13.00, which means the stock needs to trade above $403 per share for the buyer of that call to earn a profit. Even with a meaningful decline in implied volatility, the change in premiums may be more limited than is typically seen in high-volatility situations.

Because of this, call holders may be slower to unwind their positions following results, which may allow the stock to be less negatively affected by the post-opex implied volatility decline we typically see when implied volatility is extremely elevated. The same would go for put sellers unwinding their positions.

Despite the stock's recent rebound, unless implied volatility rises significantly more heading into results, the stock is likely to have a lackluster response to its earnings

Watching how the options market evolves ahead of results can be a very effective way to better understand the past earnings response beyond the headlines.

data.title: Gamma Communications Soon To Be Acquired? If So, What's A Fair Price?
data.description:
Note: The primary listing for Gamma Communications PLC (GAMCF) is the London Stock Exchange, where it is trading with GAMA as its ticker symbol. There are approx. 91 million shares freely-floated, and an average daily liquidity of over £7 million. There are risks related to the liquidity of the U.S. OTC Pink Market listing: investors that consider making an investment in the company should focus on the primary listing rather than the very illiquid U.S. listing.

---

On April 7, 2026, Gamma Communications PLC acknowledged that it has entered preliminary discussions with a number of interested parties regarding a potential sale of the company.

Under the UK Takeover Code, this triggers the start of an 'offer period' of 28 days through to May 5, 2026, by which point potential acquirers must either announce a firm offer, or withdraw from the process and renounce approaching the company again for a period of 6 months. Alternatively, Gamma may request to extent the offer period.

In its latest press release, Gamma aptly phrased this development as follows:

The Board [...] confirms that it is in preliminary discussions with a number of interested counterparties with a view to establishing whether such Potential Offerors might be willing to put forward a proposal that would deliver greater value to shareholders than pursuing a standalone independent strategy.

With that in mind, let us take this opportunity to briefly outline Gamma's business model, its current corporate strategy, recent financial performance, and, in our humble opinion, the minimum price at which the company should consider entertaining offers.

What does Gamma do?

Gamma is a leading provider of unified communications as a service (UCaaS) and connectivity solutions in the UK and a number of European markets. Founded in 2001, the company operates as a technology-driven provider of communication services, including cloud-based telephony, SIP trunking (a modern method that uses an internet connection to deliver phone services, replacing traditional physical phone lines for businesses, allowing voice, video, and messaging over a single broadband link), as well as traditional mobile services and broadband connectivity.

Let's try to explain that with a bit more clarity for people such as ourselves who aren't telecommunications experts. Paraphrasing Gamma's CEO Andrew Belshaw during the company's 2025 results presentation, he described Gamma's business as follows:

Gamma, for the last 25 years, has been a network operator. We have our own infrastructure. We have a telecoms network.

What does that actually mean? Well, fundamentally, if you want to make a phone call, if you want to dial a phone number and be connected to somebody else who is using phone numbers, you need somebody who has a telecoms network to be able to do that. And Gamma does that. It is difficult to do. We now do it in 30 countries.

Because it is regulated and because it is hard to do, all of the hyperscalers such as Cisco, Amazon, Microsoft, Ericsson LG, and Zoom, they do not do it. What we do is we take their products and we plug their solutions into our network. So everything just works. You can do video calling, you can do messaging, etc., but you can also just dial a number and make a phone call as well.

Those hyperscalers do not just talk to us because we have got a network. They also talk to us because Gamma has, around Europe, thousands of channel partners reaching tens of thousands of small- and medium-sized businesses (SMEs). Why do those businesses want to talk to Gamma? Well, fundamentally, they want to talk to us because we can deliver them the best communication solutions that are all linked into our network, so everything just works. We underpin that quality of service. It is fundamentally what Gamma is about.

Importantly, following the acquisitions of Placetel in 2024 and Starface in early 2025, the company now has the necessary scale to establish Gamma as a leading challenger in Germany, Europe's largest business communications market, which presents attractive growth opportunities as businesses there migrate to modern cloud-based platforms.

As a result of these acquisitions, the distribution of Gamma's gross profit has changed meaningfully, with a much enhanced geographical diversification and increased exposure to less mature, faster-growth markets. As shown below, for fiscal 2025, Gamma Germany represented nearly a quarter of the company's gross profits.

Another aspect of Gamma's business that is important to underscore is the recurring nature of the company's revenues, which provides stability and predictability. According to Progressive Equity Research, as of fiscal 2025, nearly 90% of Gamma's revenues are recurring sales under multi-year contracts (i.e. subscriptions & rentals, voice & data traffic, ...) while the remaining c. 10% refers to one-off sales (i.e. equipment sales, installation fees, ...).

Corporate strategy

Gamma's first strategic priority is to continue to migrate customers to modern platforms. In its home market of the UK, which is quite mature with an estimated 65% cloud adoption, the focus is on helping customers move from legacy telephony to cloud-based platforms, which reduces complexity, improves reliability, and enables flexible working, while avoiding the costs and maintenance of on-site communications hardware. Moreover, the UK’s transition from the old analogue phone network to digital, IP-based voice services by January 2027 – known as PSTN switch-off (Public Switched Telephone Network) – is accelerating the shift from copper to fiber. This is a double-edge sword for Gamma, as on the one hand it accelerates customers' migration to modern solutions, but on the other hand, this creates a one-off gross profit headwind as a result of a higher unit cost. In other words, some short-term pain for long-term gains, which was evident during fiscal 2025 and may yet last for a couple of years.

In the German market, where cloud adoption remains at an earlier stage than the UK with an estimated penetration rate of only 15-20%, recent acquisitions have provided Gamma with increased scale and a broader product portfolio to support the shift from legacy hardware to higher-value, long-term cloud subscriptions with higher recurring revenue. Overall, the company appears well positioned for significant long-term growth in this large, under-penetrated market.

Other strategic priorities include growing the core business, including via new product development, both through its own platforms and in partnership with leading global technology providers. Likewise, Gamma aims to expand into adjacent markets, mainly via organic growth but complemented by selective M&A as appropriate. This includes expanding the Managed Service offering to include customer service to channel partners, in addition to existing IoT, cybersecurity, and AI Agents solutions; as well as expanding the Service Provider business internationally. Last but not least, Gamma appears acutely aware that following a period of rapid expansion, including some M&A-driven growth, the focus ought to remain on operational efficiency and delivering exceptional customer service to clients.

Financial performance

The following graph highlights Gamma's financial performance between 2012 and 2025.

As shown below, the company has grown sales at a compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of nearly 13% to £646m in fiscal 2025, mostly via organic growth.

Profitability levels have gradually expanded over time as a result of economies of scale, the gradual transition to higher-margin products, as well as the acquisition of proprietary platforms and elimination of third-party licensing costs. Net income margins have increased from 5.6% in 2013 to 10% in 2025 (over 11.5% adjusting for exceptional items).

Using little to no financial leverage, return on equity (ROE) levels have averaged about 22% over the 2013-2025 period, well in excess of the firm's capital costs.

Importantly, cash flow generation is also strong, with cash from operations (CFO) to net income of over 140% on average between 2013 and 2025. With capital expenditures (Capex) representing about 4-5% of sales every year, this means that Gamma has been able to turn over 90% of net income into free cash flows on average.

As of the end of fiscal 2025, Gamma is in very strong financial shape, with about £50m in borrowings and lease liabilities, some £24m in cash and equivalents, and nearly £386m in shareholders' equity. This represents a net debt to equity ratio of 0.07x.

Looking at the management team and their track record of capital allocation decisions since listing, the overall picture is quite positive. The focus appears to have been mainly on reinvesting retained earnings in the business to drive organic growth, while undertaking the occasional strategic acquisition when appropriate. The company has also returned nearly 30% of net income to shareholders in the form of dividends on average, while over the past couple of years Gamma has also deployed capital to repurchase its own shares at attractive prices.

Overall, we have a clear positive view of the management team (which one can get a feel for by watching this interview from May 2025) in terms of their sector knowledge & experience, strategy, track record of capital allocation decisions, as well as incentives and remuneration. It should however be noted that Gamma's CFO, Bill Castell, has left the company in March 2026 and will be replaced by Damien Maltarp, who is currently the Group Financial Controller for the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG), and previously worked for over a decade at BT Group, where he held senior roles including CFO of BT Business (the UK B2B division) and CFO of BT Wholesale & Ventures.

Should Gamma entertain offers? If so, at what price?

Let us preface this section by stating that clearly, it is not our place to tell the company's management and Board what it should do regarding potential acquirers. And with an ownership stake of slightly less than 0.2% of the company, our (biased) two cents might be worth exactly that much.

Still, at the risk of being inappropriate, we would like to caution the company against selling itself short, considering that it is currently trading at a valuation level that is the cheapest it's ever been since listing.

In our estimation, a conservative DCF valuation exercise puts the intrinsic value of Gamma in excess of £13, while the average price target of sell-side analysts is around £14 per share.

Our modelling assumptions for the 2026-2035 period can be summed up as follows:

  • A CAGR in (organic) sales of 4.5%.
  • An net income margin averaging approx. 11%.
  • A free cash flow to net income conversion rate of over 90%.
  • A terminal growth rate of 2% and a 9% discount rate.

Stress-testing our main assumptions, and assuming that our different valuation scenarios are somewhat normally distributed, the full range of outcomes and associated probabilities is shown on the graphs below:

As shown on the right-hand side, according to our estimation, there is less than a 10% chance that the current share price of slightly more than £9 per share represents a fair valuation level for the company.

Moreover, for a takeover to make sense for the company's shareholders, relative to what they can realistically expect as long-term owners of the company under the current management team, the offer price would have to reflect a meaningful premium over the firm's intrinsic worth. Such a premium of say 20% would put the takeout value above £16 per share.

Lastly, one may also point out to the valuation multiples of the recent Starface acquisition, estimated to be around 4x EV / sales and 13x EV / adj. EBITDA. Now, Gamma as a whole may not command such premium multiples, but it certainly warrants takeout multiples that are way above the current market value (Gamma's current EV /adj. EBITDA multiple for fiscal 2026 is only about 6x according to consensus estimates, the P/E ratio is approx. 10x, and the FCF yield is approx. 9%).

Conclusion

We believe that Gamma Communications is a high-quality business currently trading at a massively undervalued price, which is a real cause for concern as potential acquirers are now circling. It is characterized by market leading positions in providing indispensable products and services to its customers, exposure to potentially fast-growing markets, a high share of recurring revenues, solid profitability and return on capital levels, strong cash flow generation, and a solid balance sheet. Additionally, the company is led by a management team that, over the last 25 years, has proven itself to be very good stewards in terms of strategy, execution, as well as disciplined capital allocation decisions. As such, for long-term shareholders, it would be a real pity to see the company be taken private at the wrong price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: McValue Pressure Makes Restaurant Brands International's Valuation Hard To Swallow
data.description:
Back in late April 2025, I rated Restaurant Brands International (QSR) as a buy, and since then the stock is up about 25%, with the total return at roughly 30%. I still think QSR is a strong company with a good game plan. However, inflationary pressure and value-oriented customers along with competition that’s increasingly focused on value will likely put pressure on QSR’s margins. While I still believe that QSR as a company is in a strong position as a business, the high valuation metrics leave me hesitant as far as investing goes.

I’ve taken a deeper look at this elsewhere, but McDonald’s struggled for quite a while with declining foot traffic and stagnant sales. They turned things around by focusing on value. Wendy’s is now making a similar pivot, following declining sales. Chipotle, rather infamously, shirked value and has been struggling. Restaurant Brands International has been among the better performers but I believe that increasingly price-conscious and value-oriented consumers will result in intense and sustained pressure on margins. I expect QSR to remain among the better performers as a company, but at the same time, the widespread industry tailwinds could weigh the stock down.

The American Consumer Might Finally Be Cracking

Consumers have proven to be quite resilient over the past year even as inflation and trade war turmoil roiled markets. Yet the war in Iran has had a major impact on consumers with energy and fuel prices spiking. The Index of Consumer Sentiment dropped to its lowest point in history in April, declining about 10.7% from the previous month. Views on current economic conditions also deteriorated, and as shown below, inflation expectations have soured as well.

YouGov, meanwhile, found that 66% of people who expect their economic conditions to worsen also plan to reduce eating out (50% expecting finances will improve also plan to cut back). Previous research has found that people under financial duress are more likely to turn to sales, promotions, and other cost-savings methods. The personal savings rate also dropped to about 4%. The savings rate has been on the decline over the last year, although did improve at the start of 2026 before once again tumbling. As savings stagnate or dwindle, we can expect consumers to respond by tightening their spending.

Researchers in Australia were taken off guard by how quickly Australian consumers changed habits. Often, habits change gradually, but many Australians started cutting spending on dining out and other luxuries pretty much immediately. The experts say this suggests that a lot of people are already living on the edge. The United States isn’t Australia, but once we get the data, I’d expect to see a similar pattern in America.

I actually think QSR could turn out to be one of the better-performing businesses amid tepid consumption. The company has already rolled out value plans for its chains and is engaging pretty heavily with digital apps and reward programs. Burger King has a $5 meal to compete with McDonald’s McValue 2.0 meals and a “Royal Perks” rewards program. Tim Horton’s has a $2-3-4 dollar menu. Popeye’s offers a $5 Faves menu.

I’ve spent a fair bit of time lurking on Reddit and other websites to try to get a feel for how people view such moves. Seems like a lot of people get annoyed by apps as they create friction. True, but at the same time, apps and reward programs help engaged customers save money with just a bit of extra work. By putting a weak gate, namely having to use an app, QSR and other restaurant chains may be able to engage with customers on tighter budgets, while people not as concerned with saving money will probably skip the app and just pay a bit more.

QSR Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

I believe QSR has, in fact, adopted the right strategy by emphasizing value, but I remain at a hold. Stock prices have climbed in recent weeks, and if QSR were in my portfolio at the moment, I’d likely cash in the profits. If my views prove correct and the need to emphasize value weighs down the stock, I’ll keep a close eye on QSR and would consider an investment if I felt the stock was bottoming out.

Burger King, Popeye’s, Firehouse Subs, and Tim Horton’s are all 98% or more franchised, according to the Franchise Times. This is important to keep in mind because franchise models tend to offload high-revenue but low-margin portions of the business onto the franchisee. Compared to a corporate chain where the central office owns all of the restaurants, you’ll often find the franchise corporate entity enjoys high profit margins, but lower revenues (per restaurant).

I’m going to compare QSR to McDonald’s (MCD), which sets a high bar in terms of profits and other metrics. I am also going to provide some stats for Wendy’s (WEN), as this franchise business has been struggling with stocks tanking. Interestingly, many of its metrics are rather similar to QSR.

MCD pulls in a very healthy 57.4% gross margin, while QSR comes in at 33.85% and Wendy’s comes in at 34.72%. QSR edges out Wendy’s in the net income margin, 8.23% to 7.86%, but MCD is far more profitable at 31.85%. Unfortunately, with the margins already rather thin (especially compared to MCD), if QSR has to lean more aggressively into promotions and deals, it could have a big impact on free cash flows. Where QSR really shines is in revenue growth, with YOY weighing in at 12.23%. MCD grew by just 3.72%, while WEN shrank by 3.1% (a big part of why its stock has tanked).

The TTM GAAP PE ratio is also rather high at 29.83, edging out MCD’s 26 PE. Wendy’s shines in this department with an 8.18 PE. MCD has an exceptionally high Price/Sales value at 8.26 (TTM), while QSR is more reasonable at 2.73, and WEN is extremely cheap at .62. These numbers suggest to me that QSR is a bit expensive, but the growth is encouraging. If not for the larger macro headwinds discussed in the last section, I might be willing to take on the higher price.

A quick reverse DCF calculation shows that QSR will need to secure 13% levered free cash flow growth to justify its current share price. This is based on an investor hurdle rate of 10%. The growth period was set to 5 years and the terminal growth rate was set to 4% to reflect global GDP growth. Note: QSR has over 100 million outstanding class B partnership units, which are not counted in some automated marketcap calculations. In this case, I used the full implied shares, which stand at 455.86 million.

In the right economic environment, 13% would strike me as pretty reasonable. Yet I believe headwinds will mount. I’m not worried about QSR’s long-term prospects and if I get the feeling that the company is bottoming out in the future, I could see my rating changing to a buy rather quickly. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools are more optimistic right now, giving a buy rating, but my fellow analysts in aggregate result in a hold rating. I also assign a hold rating.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Markets have been surging over the last few days, stemming from optimism regarding the prospects of peace in the Middle East. The economy has, overall, been sending a lot of mixed signals in my opinion. Yet my cynicism could be misplaced, and admittedly, there are lots of positive signals, like low unemployment and sustained GDP growth. If the economy maintains strength, and better yet, improves, it could ease pressure on consumers. QSR is in pretty solid shape as a company, so if discretionary spending can be boosted, QSR could hit the needed levered FCF growth and otherwise enjoy success.

Key Takeaway: I’m At Hold For Now But I’ll Keep Watch

QSR isn’t on my buy list for now. That’s nothing against the company but rather stemming from my concerns regarding the economy. If the economy starts to improve or QSR’s share prices decline, I could see myself rerating as a buy pretty quickly. If I held QSR in my portfolio, I would be tempted to sell. At the very least, I'd keep a close eye on the ongoing fast food value wars, and if discounts got substantially more aggressive, I would reevaluate.

data.title: The Dollar Is Forecasting Tougher Times Ahead - EUR/USD, AUD/USD And Dollar Index Overview
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US dollar (DXY) has corrected quite severely since the announcement of the two-week ceasefire, and not without good cause.

The infamous Petrodollar trade has gripped financial markets on all sides since the beginning of the US-Iran-Israel conflict, particularly amid the rise in crude oil to 4-year highs.

The USD has historically held a decent correlation with Black Gold, but the latest wave of panic during the conflict re-strengthened the bonds between the two financial assets, rowing the same boat.

With markets ever so ecstatic about a US-Iran deal and the fact that the war is not extending much longer than originally priced, this led to an explosion to all-time highs in stock markets, a swift drop in oil prices, and, consequently, a tumble in the US dollar.

This came shortly after a daily double top in the global reserve currency, which was nice enough to mark the bearish pattern indicating a turn in how markets viewed the war.

But after a 2.50% correction, the US dollar has seemingly done correcting. So, if the dollar forecasted the truce, could it now be forecasting tougher times ahead?

The issue with the narrative is that the ceasefire is ending tomorrow, and a US delegation, including Vice President J.D. Vance, is struggling to coordinate its departure amid mixed messaging from the Iranian side.

As the US President said, he does not want to extend the ceasefire, and without a deal, we're going straight back to the bombs. So, FX markets could be feeling the turn.

The US dollar is leading all other FX currencies, but the Kiwi dollar is supported by an NZ CPI beat and the repricing for a hike at the upcoming meeting.

While the changes are small, it is now the second consecutive day of a greenback rebound, so traders will have to pay close attention.

We will look at the Dollar Index, EUR/USD, and AUD/USD to assess the current state of the market and where to look next.

Dollar Index 4H Chart

The US dollar has now attempted, and failed, to break the 98.00 major support for the third time during the morning action.

These levels hold right in the middle of its larger time frame range, which implies a general lack of conviction from bears that the dollar should already erase its war gains.

Now testing its 4H 50-period MA, a key technical indicator for the prior coming, FX markets will be facing a test:

  • Breaking above it (98.40) would hint at a bullish rebound ahead, which confirms above 98.70 (if the war picks up again).
  • On the other hand, rejecting 98.00 continues the bearish path for the US dollar.

Levels of interest for the Dollar Index:

Resistance Levels

  • 98.335 4H 50-period MA (bullish above)
  • 98.50-98.70 war pivot
  • 99.40-99.50 resistance
  • Initial war spike 99.68
  • Weekly range highs 100.00
  • 100.00-100.50 main resistance zone
  • War highs 100.544

Support Levels

  • 98.00 2025 support (testing - bearish below)
  • Support 97.40-97.60
  • 2025 lows 96.40-96.80 support

AUD/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

AUD/USD is taking somewhat of a lead, bouncing from the test of its upward channel bottom in recent action.

A break above 0.71860 (March highs) would continue the bullish path ahead, and if the channel was to hold (implying peace), a rally to 0.7250 could occur.

Nevertheless, the rebound attempt seems for now quite shy, hence the importance of the March high level. Failing to reject it could lead to a break of the bull channel.

Levels of interest for AUD/USD:

Resistance Levels

  • 2023 highs from 0.7140-0.7160 resistance (broken)
  • 0.71867 March highs
  • June 2022 extremes 0.72-0.7230
  • Channel highs 0.7250

Support Levels

  • 0.7150 channel lows
  • 4H 50-period MA - 0.71280
  • 0.6970-0.70 major pivot
  • 0.69-0.6935 early Feb support
  • 0.68340 war lows

EUR/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD is showing sharply similar signs as the Dollar Index (naturally, in reverse), testing its 4H 50-period MA this time as support.

Bears did take the upper hand at the beginning of the week, rejecting sharply the test of the 1.1850 resistance and now trading close to 1,000 pips below.

Breaking below the MA hints at further downside, with confirmation below 1.17200.

Levels to place on your EUR/USD charts:

Resistance Levels

  • Resistance zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)
  • 1.1830 June 2025 highs
  • 1.1850-1.1860 recent test
  • Sep 2021 highs - Resistance 1.19-1.1950 zone

Support Levels

  • 1.1760 4H 50-period MA
  • 1.17-1.1720 March pivot
  • Rebound highs 1.17200 (bearish below)
  • Major pivot 1.16250-1.16350
  • 1.1540-1.1570 war support
  • 1.1475-1.15 November support
  • War lows 1.1410

Safe trades, and keep a close eye on ceasefire news!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Compass Pathways: Red Hot Psychedelic Market For Pioneer With Near Term Psychedelic Filing In TRD
data.description:
This is my fifth COMPASS Pathways plc (CMPS) article after most recently, 01/08/2026's "Compass Pathways: Exciting Prospects For This Psychedelic Pioneer With Significant Risks". In Exciting Prospects, I rated Compass as a "Buy"; thanks to recent news from the White House discussed below it has seen a nice pop rising about 25% since publication of Exciting Prospects.

In this article I explain why I am reiterating my "Buy".

Compass's pipeline development is proceeding apace.

In its latest 10-K, Compass provides an overview of its business. With an initial focus on treatment-resistant depression [TRD], Compass's goal was to accelerate:

...patient access to evidence-based innovation in mental health...[It is] pioneering a new paradigm for treating mental health conditions focused on rapid and durable responses through the development of ...[its] investigational COMP360 psilocybin treatment, potentially a first in class treatment. COMP360 is ...[its] proprietary psilocybin formulation that includes ...[its] pharmaceutical-grade polymorphic crystalline psilocybin, optimized for stability and purity.

Towards that end in 2018, it received FDA's Breakthrough Therapy designation for COMP360 in the treatment of TRD. In 11/2021 it announced positive results from its phase 2b trial which was written up in an 11/2022 article in NEJM. In 2025, it commenced a pivotal trial program for COMP360. It consisted of:

  1. Pivotal trial 1 (COMP005) (n=258): a single dose (25mg) monotherapy compared with placebo.
  2. Pivotal trial 2 (COMP006) (n=581): a fixed repeat dose monotherapy using three dose arms: 25mg, 10mg and 1mg. This trial is designed to investigate whether a second dose can increase therapeutic response.
  3. The primary endpoint in both pivotal trials was the change from baseline in the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale, or MADRS, total score at week 6.

In 06/2025 results from COMP005 demonstrated that its psilocybin treatment met its primary endpoint. Subsequently in 02/2026 Compass:

...announced the successful achievement of the primary endpoint in our ongoing Phase 3 COMP006 trial and Part B results from our ongoing Phase 3 COMP005 trial. We reported that the COMP006 trial achieved its primary endpoint with two fixed doses, administered 3 weeks apart, of COMP360 25 mg versus 1 mg demonstrating a highly statistically significant reduction in symptom severity with a p-value of <0.001 and a clinically meaningful difference of -3.8 points in change at six weeks.

Its next step on 02/08/2026 was to request a meeting with the FDA to discuss a rolling submission of its planned NDA.

New policy from president could turbocharge COMP360 with FDA.

Midday on 04/16/2026, CBS reported that the White House was expected to soon sign an executive order that sent psychedelic stocks dramatically higher. For example CMPS opened the day at $5.97; after rising to $6.75, it closed at $6.66 on ~4X volume compared to recent trading days.

Just 2 days later on 04/18/2026, more welcome news came from the White House; President Donald Trump issued an executive order. He touted the order as likely to dramatically accelerate access to treatments based on psychedelic drugs such as such as psilocybin, ibogaine, LSD, and MDMA.

HHS Secretary Kennedy and FDA Commissioner Makary were at the signing ceremony. Kennedy noted that the Order would:

...accelerate research, approval, and access to new mental health treatments, including psychedelic therapies.

Makary advised that the FDA was going to issue three national priority vouchers for psychedelics. He noted that under this voucher program:

...in this administration, drugs can get approved in weeks—not a year or a year plus, but in weeks—if they are in line with our national priorities.

This sounds a bit hyperbolic and a bit scary, as my mind roles back to the thalidomide crisis in the late '50s and early '60s. The US was spared this agony by the courage and thoroughness of an FDA examiner who questioned its safety. The FDA has generally been a good steward of the nation's health. I am cautiously optimistic that it will continue to be so under its new administration, despite populist pressures to act quickly for favored therapies.

Compass has significant upcoming near term catalysts.

Slide 3 below of Compass's latest investor's presentation dated 03/2026 titled Investment Highlights includes a bunch of critical details for investors:

In this article I am focusing on its lead TRD indication, not on its second indication for post traumatic stress disorder [PTSD]. As shown on the first panel TRD has a significantly sized ~4 million patient population which is barely served by existing therapies.

The third panel on panel Slide 3 advises that Compass is waiting for data expected early in Q3/2026. It expects that it will submit its NDA in Q4/2026. The FDA should accept or reject the filing within 60 days of the NDA filing. If it is accepted for accelerated review a decision would then be targeted within 6 months, reduced from standard 10 months review.

In other words big news should be coming out for Compass spread over the balance of 2026 and during 2027.

Compass has strong liquidity with cash runway into 2028; financing needed to fund launch.

The fourth panel on Compass's slide 3 above shows its liquidity position and its cash runway. It reads:

$150 million public offering and exercise of $200 million in warrants completed in February 2026 extending runway into 2028.

While this is truly a strong cash position; it raises the near certainty of additional financings sooner rather than later. As discussed above, if all goes according to plan the FDA will act on its TRD NDA in mid to late 2027. Its projected cash runway only runs into 2028. Accordingly it will need to raise significant additional funds to cover its launch of COMP360.

Its latest available cash burn as shown by its 10-K net cash used in operating activities for 2025 is ~$0.157 billion. This figure could be expected to increase significantly as Compass prepares for its COMP360 TRD launch. Accordingly shareholders should expect Compass's discouraging trajectory of share dilution to reduce existing shareholder’s percentage ownership even more than as shown below:

Ouch! According to this graph share count has risen by a factor of 4.2 over the last three years. Please note this chart does not reflect Monday, 04/20/2026's jump in price ranging from $8.64 to $10.21.

Compass is a high risk "Buy" for biotech investor's able to sustain significant losses.

Compass carried a market cap of ~$0.858 billion as I wrote on 04/19/2026. Shares took another ~35% leap on 04/20/2026 boosting market cap close to $1.2 billion. This is certainly not a screaming "Buy". Compass is entirely speculative. It has no revenues; as stated in its 10-K it has never had an FDA approved therapy.

It does have a likely near term NDA filing for a new politically favored and potentially powerful psychedelic drug franchise. Investors need to be cautious here with Compass stock having moved so fast over the last three trading days. That said, I maintain my "Buy" rating for Compass for reasons stated above.

Conclusion

Investors who have positions in Compass that they would like to increase will likely be watching carefully and buying on pullbacks. While tactics will vary in this regard, the strategy should be to buy Compass as it moves forward.

data.title: Equity Bancshares: A High-Return Bank Trading At A Deep Discount
data.description:
In a market that has largely written off small-cap banks as rate-sensitive, low-growth balance sheet stories, Equity Bancshares (EQBK) stands out as a mispriced outlier. The stock trades like a typical community bank, yet the underlying business is evolving into something far more dynamic.

EQBK a disciplined acquirer with a proven integration playbook. It has a growing organic engine and returns that already rival higher-multiple peers. The market seems to be missing the earnings power embedded in the company’s model as integration headwinds fade and synergies take hold.

As shown below, EQBK has seen a decent 26% run over the past 12 months, and yet still trades at a low forward P/E of just 9x. In this article, I highlight EQBK including recent business results, and discuss why this underfollowed stock may be a long-term winner from here, so let’s get started!

A Community Bank With a Strategic Edge

Equity Bancshares operates as a traditional community banking franchise across the Midwest. It provides commercial lending, deposit services, and a growing portfolio of fee-based products. On the surface, it looks like a standard regional bank. Underneath, however, the strategy is more nuanced.

Management has spent years building a repeatable model centered on acquiring under-optimized community bank and integrating them quickly. It then layers in organic growth through commercial relationships and cross-selling.

The recent acquisition of Frontier is a textbook example, which drove a 20% increase in assets and contributed to record quarterly revenue. It immediately scaled the balance sheet while setting up future growth levers. As shown below, EQBK has more than tripled its total assets over the past 10 years alone.

Recent Earnings: Strong Results And Even Stronger Core Trends

The most recent Q1’26 results show why the stock looks undervalued relative to its earnings trajectory. Adjusted earnings came in at $1.23 per share, up sequentially, while core return on tangible common equity reached 16.1%. That level of profitability already places the company in the upper tier of regional banks, yet it is not reflected in the valuation.

Digging deeper, net interest income increased meaningfully. This was supported by a sharp rise in earning assets following the Frontier acquisition. While net interest margin declined modestly to 4.33%, this was expected due to the higher-cost funding inherited from Frontier. On a normalized basis, margin was closer to 4.29%, right in line with expectations and suggesting stability rather than deterioration.

Importantly, EQBK’s efficiency ratio improved over the past 2 quarters to 56.68%, as shown below. This is calculated by taking non-interest expenses divided by net revenue. As such, a lower ratio signals that EQBK is being more cost efficient through increased operating leverage.

Loan growth and fee income trends point to a strengthening core franchise. Loan production reached $267 million, up over 20% sequentially, with pipelines continuing to build. At the same time, fee income is becoming more diversified, with growth in debit card, mortgage, insurance, and wealth management revenues. Taken together, the quarter reflects a business that is temporarily absorbing integration noise while simultaneously expanding its long-term earnings base.

Forward Outlook: A Setup for Earnings Expansion

Looking ahead, the case for upside rests on a combination of internal execution and favorable industry dynamics. Management reaffirmed $5 EPS target for 2026, representing 19% growth from last year. This is to be driven by a mix of cost synergies, margin normalization, and organic growth.

Drivers of this outlook include deposit repricing and balance sheet optimization, which should lift margins. The Frontier acquisition initially pressured funding costs, but those same deposits represent a clear opportunity. As they are repriced or replaced with lower-cost core deposits, margin expansion should follow.

Second, organic growth is accelerating. The company is hiring bankers in new markets and expanding commercial relationships. This is supported by a loan pipeline that already exceeds $500 million. Management expects mid-single-digit organic loan growth, which combined with operating leverage, should drive earnings higher.

Plus, fee income is underpenetrated. Treasury management, mortgage, and wealth management services are being expanded across newly acquired markets where these offerings were previously limited. This creates a multi-year runway for noninterest income growth.

From an industry perspective, the environment is becoming more favorable for well-run regional banks. Stabilizing interest rates reduce uncertainty around margins. At the same time, consolidation continues to create opportunities for disciplined acquirers like EQBK.

Balance Sheet, Dividends, and Valuation

EQBK maintains a solid balance sheet with a CET1 ratio of 11.5% and has a loan-to-deposit ratio of 86%. It currently yields 1.6% and the dividend is well-covered by a low 15% payout ratio. While the yield is low, EQBK hikes the dividend at a far higher rate than the S&P 500 (SPY), with a 20% increase just last year.

I see value in EQBK at the current price of $45.55 with a forward P/E of just 9.2x, sitting well under its historical P/E of 18.7, as shown below. With analysts 6-19% EPS growth annually over the next two years, EQBK could deliver market-beating total returns.

Risks

Integration is the most immediate concern. While Equity Bancshares has executed well historically, the Frontier deal is still being absorbed. Temporary disruptions include the increase in past-due loans tied to process changes. Management has characterized these issues as transitional rather than structural, but execution will need to remain strong.

Credit risk is another factor to watch. While current trends are stable and below historical norms, the bank operates in markets that could be sensitive to economic slowdown. The acquired portfolio appears well underwritten, but any deterioration in credit quality could impact earnings.

Competition is also intensifying. Management acknowledged a competitive market, particularly in loan pricing. While discipline has been maintained, aggressive competitors could pressure spreads or limit growth opportunities.

Investor Takeaway

Equity Bancshares is not being valued for what it is becoming. The market sees a small-cap regional bank dealing with integration noise. The reality is a high-return franchise with a proven acquisition engine, growing organic momentum, and multiple near-term catalysts for earnings expansion.

The recent quarter already demonstrates the earnings power of the platform, even before full synergies are realized. Margin headwinds should reverse and cost savings materialize. Plus, as organic growth builds, the gap between price and intrinsic value should narrow.

Lastly, for investors willing to look past short-term integration effects, the setup is straightforward. EQBK is a bank generating mid-teens returns, growing earnings, and trading at an attractive discount. If management executes as it has in the past, that discount is unlikely to persist.

data.title: Mitsubishi Heavy Industries: Big Deal, Expensive Stock
data.description:
Thesis

This is the third time I’m covering Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHVYF), a large-cap company that works across different areas like energy, defense, and infrastructure. Fortunately, I bought the stock after my first “Buy” recommendation. And the last time I touched this, I knocked it down to a "Hold." My thoughts were that the price ran far ahead of the business, and the numbers didn’t fully support it.

In this update, after I noticed the stock ticker was getting more attention due to a deal between Australia and Japan, and after taking a closer look at the details, I’m still choosing to remain neutral. I think that while the deal strengthens the long-term story, the current price already reflects a lot of that optimism.

Paid Upfront, Pressure Ahead

From my angle, the Australia–Mitsubishi Heavy Industries deal is a big step forward for Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, as they are building more than just vessels for Japan and are now winning overseas defense contracts. The governments of Australia and Japan have confirmed that Mitsubishi Heavy Industries will build the first Mogami-class ships (a multi-role stealth frigate, built to hunt submarines, target ships, and handle air-defense duties) in Japan, and then move production later to Western Australia. This is a working contract with a delivery timeframe, including responsibility for assisting Australia in building the vessels and generating revenue for several years after construction starts. According to Reuters and the Australian Government, this is the most significant arms export from Japan in several decades.

  • Revenue (Q1 FY2025): JPY 1,193.6 billion
  • Business profit (Q1 FY2025): ~JPY 100.0 billion
  • YoY revenue growth: +7%
  • YoY business profit growth: +25%
  • Order intake (Q1 FY2025): JPY 1,768.6 billion
  • Order backlog: JPY 10.7729 trillion

Timing is important here for Mitsubishi Heavy Industries because it had already reported a strong quarter before this partnership was announced. Its Energy Systems division helped drive revenue growth by continuing to work through and deliver on orders it had already received. The Plants and Infrastructure division turned in a solid showing, helped by projects that actually got done and a steady stream of demand to swap out aging facilities. The Aircraft, Defense, and Space division also experienced growth; however, this was due to continued progress on previously booked contracts rather than newly awarded contracts. When management talked on the earnings call, they basically said: yes, new defense orders were slower than last year, but that's fine because the work they already had booked was moving through production and getting delivered.

  • Free cash flow: JPY 64.3 billion
  • Operating cash flow: JPY 89.6 billion
  • Operating cash flow (FY2025 9M): JPY 256.7 billion

The cash flows support this. Customers are paying deposits earlier and faster, which is helping Mitsubishi Heavy Industries improve its operating cash flow. In simple terms, the company is receiving cash before it spends money to complete the work. This effect is especially noticeable in its gas turbine and defense businesses. That's a good sign of customer confidence, but it comes with strings attached. From there, Mitsubishi Heavy has now committed to delivering more. The profitability of their contracts moving forward will depend on their ability to deliver on time, manage material costs, and avoid cost overruns (especially toward the end of large industrial contracts).

  • Aircraft, Defense & Space revenue (FY2025 9M): JPY 891.3 billion
  • Aircraft, Defense & Space segment profit: JPY 105.4 billion

The Aussie frigate program may not only help grow the overall defense industry but could improve how it operates on an ongoing basis as well. With more opportunities opening overseas, they should be able to produce more ships using the same factories. That means higher output, spreading fixed costs over a larger number of ships and keeping production lines humming. Keep in mind, Japan has recently eased its rules on exporting defense equipment, allowing companies like Mitsubishi Heavy Industries to increase production volume, resulting in lower unit costs and greater industrial capacity.

That said, this is where you have to pump the brakes a little. When mixing industrial targets with political considerations, international defense contracts often face difficulties. Australia has set a timetable for when the warships should be delivered. In addition, they're looking for opportunities to build a portion of the warships in Australia, while also allowing Australian businesses and workers to be part of the whole build process. Mitsubishi Heavy has to create a collaborative global supply chain involving suppliers in both Japan and Australia, as well as transfer technical know-how across borders and achieve a reasonable profit margin. The official Australian government announcement makes clear that maintaining the ships, training the workforce, and building local operating capability are all part of the deal, which means this is a much harder job than just shipping finished frigates to Sydney.

  • Energy Systems revenue (FY2025 9M): JPY 1,354.8 billion
  • Energy Systems segment profit: JPY 146.8 billion
  • Order intake (FY2025 9M): JPY 5,029.1 billion
  • Revenue (FY2025 9M): JPY 3,326.9 billion
  • Business profit (FY2025 9M): JPY 301.2 billion
  • Discontinued operations profit/loss: -JPY 11.8 billion

One final note here: energy is still one of the main drivers for this company; a thick backlog and a steady stream of gas turbine deliveries are carrying most of the load. In fact, Reuters previously reported that management is confident demand for gas turbines will hold up, which is translating into better revenue quality and more consistent profit margins. Even so, management also acknowledged that not all divisions within the company are firing on all cylinders. Some divisions have posted growth, while others have performed at a steady pace. With Mitsubishi Heavy still carrying discontinued operations on its books, they still have some cleaning up to do in their portfolio. It's not all polished and perfect yet.

Growth Priced In

I’m used to seeing graphs like the one above, but mostly in the AI or tech-adjacent space, so at first glance, the valuation is already hard to justify using anything like a normal earnings framework. Here, FAST Graphs shows a blended P/E of 50.80x against both a fair value ratio of 15.00x and a normal P/E of 29.20x, which, again, just confirms to me the market's capitalizing a lot more than steady industrial growth.

And the forecasting view is much less forgiving. With a fair value of $10.67 compared to a current price of $28.89, and an estimated annual return that screens negative from here.

Seeking Alpha points in the same direction. EV/EBITDA at 26.90x, EV/EBIT at 40.12x. And price-to-book at 5.47x all sit well above sector medians. So this isn’t just a case of one distorted multiple.

If we look ahead, revenue, EBIT, EBITDA, and operating cash flow all trend in the right direction. And the order for three Mogami frigates helps a lot. But the company doesn’t pay much in dividends (see FAST Graphs' dividend yield above), its stock price is high compared to the cash it makes, and there are common risks in its industry like delays in shipbuilding, timing issues with defense contracts, and challenges turning revenue into profit.

Final Verdict

Hold. I'm holding onto my shares but not adding more here. I still see a path for growth. The backlog is in a strong position, and there is clear potential for growth if defense sales continue to increase in international markets. That said, the stock already feels pretty expensive.
